role_summary: NAACP Legal Defense Fund Director-Counsel 1961-1984, succeeding Thurgood Marshall; co-counsel on Brown v. Board of Education (1954) and arguing counsel in Meredith v. Fair (1962); Vice Dean of Columbia Law School.
ai_reading: Greenberg's position on the Sacred vs Secular Framing axis is -0.82, far toward Sacred Framing. The cultural placement of Greenberg, a Columbia Law-trained constitutional litigator who argued nearly forty civil rights cases at the Supreme Court and ran the NAACP Legal Defense Fund for twenty-three years, would have predicted the opposite pole; secular-legal vocabulary is the institutional dialect of his career. The embedding reads his oral history as Sacred-side instead, possibly catching the moral-witness framing his memoir Crusaders in the Courts deploys, or the church-network testimony embedded in the civil-rights cases his litigation built on. His top semantic neighbor is Robert L. Carter (entry 108, count 258), the NAACP LDF lawyer who preceded him at the organization, a pairing the cultural record predicts and that the embedding confirms. The Sacred-Framing axis position is the surprise.
biographical_paragraph: Greenberg succeeded Thurgood Marshall as Director-Counsel of the NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund in 1961 and held the post for twenty-three years, until 1984 [src: 2]. The position followed his 1954 work as co-counsel on Brown v. Board of Education; he argued Meredith v. Fair before the Supreme Court in 1962, the case that produced James Meredith's enrollment at the University of Mississippi [src: 2]. Across his LDF tenure he argued roughly forty civil rights cases before the Supreme Court [src: 2]. His memoir Crusaders in the Courts appeared in 1994 [src: 3]. From 1989 to 1993 he served as Dean of Columbia College and was later Vice Dean of Columbia Law School and the Alphonse Fletcher Jr. Professor of Law Emeritus [src: 2]. Born December 22, 1924 in Brooklyn, New York, Greenberg died October 12, 2016 at ninety-one [src: 2]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 56 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].

photo.alt: President Barack Obama greets Jack Greenberg, former NAACP Legal Defense Fund director-counsel, and his wife Deborah Greenberg in the Blue Room of the White House, May 16, 2014
photo.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via the Executive Office of the President)
photo.license: Public domain (work of an employee of the Executive Office of the President)

movement_context: The Brown v. Board of Education litigation Greenberg joined as co-counsel was a consolidation of five cases the NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund had been building since the late 1940s under Thurgood Marshall's direction; Greenberg was twenty-eight when he was assigned the Delaware companion case Belton v. Gebhart, the only one of the five in which the lower court had ruled for the Black plaintiffs and ordered immediate desegregation, a holding the U.S. Supreme Court affirmed in the unanimous May 17, 1954 opinion that became Brown v. Board of Education, 347 U.S. 483 [src: 2, 4]. The LDF was a separate 501(c)(3) tax-exempt entity that the NAACP had spun off in 1940 to preserve the parent organization's lobbying capacity while protecting LDF's litigation budget; the organizational separation Greenberg's tenure would make permanent began as an accounting and tax-exemption distinction and grew into a full institutional split [src: 2, 5]. President John F. Kennedy nominated Marshall to the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit on September 23, 1961; the Senate confirmed him on September 11, 1962 after an eleven-month delay caused by Southern senators' procedural opposition, but Marshall began his transition immediately, and the LDF board elected Greenberg as director-counsel that same fall [src: 2, 5]. Greenberg's twenty-three-year LDF tenure produced the litigation that built out the constitutional framework Brown had only sketched: the Loving v. Virginia, 388 U.S. 1 (1967) striking-down of state anti-miscegenation statutes; Furman v. Georgia, 408 U.S. 238 (1972), the five-to-four decision that halted U.S. capital punishment for the four years before Gregg v. Georgia reinstated it; and the Title VII employment-discrimination doctrine the LDF built case by case through Local 189, United Papermakers and Paperworkers v. United States, 416 F.2d 980 (5th Cir. 1969) and Griggs v. Duke Power Co., 401 U.S. 424 (1971), the unanimous opinion that established the disparate-impact theory under Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 [src: 2, 4, 5]. The 1980 dispute that produced the permanent NAACP-LDF separation, the NAACP under Benjamin L. Hooks sued the LDF over use of the NAACP name and the federal District Court for the District of Columbia ruled in 1985 that the LDF could continue using the name on grounds of accrued institutional distinctness, was litigated through the final years of Greenberg's tenure and resolved after his 1984 departure [src: 2, 5].
legacy_and_reception: Greenberg left the LDF in 1984 to teach full time at Columbia Law School, where he had been an adjunct since the 1950s, and served as Dean of Columbia College from 1989 to 1993; he subsequently held the Alphonse Fletcher Jr. Professor of Law chair and the Vice Dean position at Columbia Law School through the 2000s [src: 2, 6]. His 1994 Basic Books memoir Crusaders in the Courts: How a Dedicated Band of Lawyers Fought for the Civil Rights Revolution is the principal first-person institutional history of the LDF litigation campaign across the Marshall and Greenberg years; the book reconstructs the Brown litigation strategy, the Meredith v. Fair, 372 U.S. 916 (1962) University of Mississippi desegregation case Greenberg argued at the Supreme Court, and the Furman and Griggs litigation he supervised as director-counsel [src: 3]. The Library of Congress holds the institutional record of the litigation in two parallel collections: the Thurgood Marshall Papers (Manuscript Division, MSS 36486; finding aid 1996) cover the 1936-1961 LDF years before Greenberg's succession, and the NAACP Records (Manuscript Division, the parent-organization papers covering both the NAACP and LDF through 1965) cover the institutional context within which the LDF operated [src: 6]. Greenberg died of pancreatic cancer at ninety-one on October 12, 2016 at his home in Manhattan; the New York Times obituary by Robert D. McFadden and the Columbia Law School memorial documented his role as the second director-counsel and the longest-serving member of the LDF inner circle from the Brown era [src: 6, 7].

gallery.alt: George E. C. Hayes, Thurgood Marshall, and James Nabrit Jr. celebrating the Brown v. Board of Education victory on the U.S. Supreme Court steps, May 17, 1954
gallery.caption: The three LDF lawyers who argued the consolidated Brown v. Board of Education, 347 U.S. 483 (1954), celebrating the unanimous opinion on the U.S. Supreme Court steps on May 17, 1954: from left, George Edward Chalmer Hayes (Howard University Law School, lead counsel on the District of Columbia companion case Bolling v. Sharpe), Thurgood Marshall (LDF director-counsel, lead counsel on the consolidated cases), and James Nabrit Jr. (Howard University Law School). Greenberg, then twenty-eight, was co-counsel on the Delaware companion case Belton v. Gebhart and the youngest member of the LDF Brown team. Wire-service photograph from the New York World-Telegram and Sun collection at the Library of Congress.
gallery.photographer: Unknown (New York World-Telegram and Sun staff)
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (sourced from Library of Congress, New York World-Telegram and Sun collection)
gallery.license: Public domain (Library of Congress: no known copyright restrictions)

gallery.alt: George E. C. Hayes, Thurgood Marshall, and James Nabrit Jr. conferring during the Brown v. Board of Education argument, 1953
gallery.caption: Hayes, Marshall, and Nabrit conferring during the December 1953 reargument of Brown v. Board of Education before the U.S. Supreme Court. The case was first argued in December 1952; Chief Justice Earl Warren ordered reargument on the historical question of the Fourteenth Amendment's framers' intent, the reargument that this photograph captures. Greenberg participated in the brief-writing and reargument preparation as co-counsel on the Delaware Belton v. Gebhart companion case. Wire-service photograph from the New York World-Telegram and Sun collection at the Library of Congress.

sources.title: NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund institutional history
sources.publisher: NAACP Legal Defense Fund organizational record (the institutional history of LDF from its 1940 spin-off from the NAACP parent organization through the Marshall and Greenberg director-counsel eras)

sources.title: Jack Greenberg, Crusaders in the Courts: How a Dedicated Band of Lawyers Fought for the Civil Rights Revolution
sources.publisher: Basic Books, 1994; WorldCat OCLC 29360571 (Greenberg's first-person memoir of the LDF litigation campaign across his 1949 hire as a staff attorney through his 1984 departure as director-counsel)

sources.title: Mark V. Tushnet, Making Civil Rights Law: Thurgood Marshall and the Supreme Court, 1936-1961
sources.publisher: Oxford University Press, 1994; WorldCat OCLC 30113930 (the standard institutional history of the NAACP LDF litigation campaign Marshall directed during the years Greenberg served as a staff attorney and assistant counsel)

sources.title: Mark V. Tushnet, The NAACP's Legal Strategy Against Segregated Education, 1925-1950
sources.publisher: University of North Carolina Press, 1987; WorldCat OCLC 13643191 (the prequel volume documenting the pre-Brown LDF case-selection and graduate-school precedent-building strategy that Greenberg joined as a junior staff attorney in 1949)

sources.title: Brown v. Board of Education of Topeka, 347 U.S. 483 (1954)
sources.publisher: U.S. Supreme Court (unanimous opinion of the Court, Warren, C.J.; the consolidated school-desegregation precedent the LDF won and Greenberg co-litigated as counsel on the Delaware Belton v. Gebhart companion case)

sources.title: Griggs v. Duke Power Co., 401 U.S. 424 (1971)
sources.publisher: U.S. Supreme Court (unanimous opinion of the Court, Burger, C.J.; the Title VII disparate-impact precedent the LDF built under Greenberg's director-counselship)

sources.title: Furman v. Georgia, 408 U.S. 238 (1972)
sources.publisher: U.S. Supreme Court (per curiam, five-to-four; the death-penalty moratorium opinion the LDF won under Greenberg's director-counselship, halting U.S. capital punishment for four years until Gregg v. Georgia reinstated it in 1976)

sources.title: Jack Greenberg, Civil Rights Lawyer at Forefront of Battles, Dies at 91
sources.publisher: The New York Times, October 13, 2016 (obituary by Robert D. McFadden; documents Greenberg's October 12, 2016 death from pancreatic cancer at his Manhattan home and the Columbia Law School memorial record)
